data.title: Axalta: Underwhelming Results, An Equally Underwhelming Potential Merger
data.description:
For a company that I had thought was making progress, Axalta Coating Systems Ltd.’s (AXTA) performance since my last update has been disappointing. Weakness in the company’s served end markets certainly hasn’t helped, but I expected more from this company. More disappointing still was management’s decision to enter into what was effectively a no-premium merger with a lackluster player (Akzo Nobel (AKZOY)) where synergies could well disappoint.

Axalta shares are down about 5% since that last article, underperforming all of its peers in what has admittedly been a rougher period for specialty chemical companies due to cost inflation and weaker end-market demand.

The deal with Akzo is not yet finalized, and both shareholder and regulatory approvals are pending. At this point, I would say the likelihood of a competing bid is low, though the valuation is not prohibitive to a higher bid. I do believe multiple end markets will improve in 2026 and that there could be some upside to the combined companies, but my lack of confidence in Akzo management tamps down whatever long-term enthusiasm I might have.

A Disappointing Q4… And I Have Some Questions

Axalta reported weaker-than-expected fourth-quarter results, with misses at the revenue and earnings lines, as well as soft guidance for the upcoming year.

Revenue fell 4% as reported and more than 7% in organic terms, missing by around 1%. Gross margin weakened 70 bp to 33.4%, and overall price/mix (down 0.4%) was not helpful. Adjusted EBITDA declined 1% (margin up 60 bp to 21.6%), missing by 4%, while operating income declined 3% (margin up 10 bp to 16.5%), missing by 6%.

Performance revenue declined 10% in organic terms, with double-digit declines in Refinish and a high single-digit decline in Industrial. Segment EBITDA fell 9%, with margin down 70 bp to 22.8%.

Mobility revenue fell about 3% in organic terms, with light vehicles down low single-digits organically and commercial vehicles down low single-digits as well. EBITDA rose 20%, with margin up almost three points to 19.4%.

PPG’s (PPG) Performance Coatings business saw 3% organic growth in the quarter, with a high single-digit decline in refinish, double-digit growth in aerospace and protective & marine, and low single-digit growth in traffic solutions. PPG’s Industrial Coatings business grew 4% organically, with flat industrial sales, double-digit growth in packaging, and low single-digit growth in auto OEM.

Akzo reported low single-digit contraction in its Marine & Protective business, flat Automotive & Specialty (with strong aero), and a mid-single-digit decline in Industrial, with “weak” packaging.

I’m not surprised about the weakness in auto refinishing given market dynamics (insurance claims, pricing pressures, et al.). The auto OEM performance is more disappointing given slight production growth in the fourth quarter (up around 1% per S&P Global) and Axalta’s comparative success with Chinese LV OEMs.

The weakness in Industrial is more surprising and disappointing to me. Multiple industrials have reported better results in the fourth quarter, and a high single-digit decline is weak relative to its rivals. Some of this could be a mix (a bigger skew to appliances and so on), but it’s an area of concern going into 2026.

Merging With Akzo Is Far From Compelling

The biggest recent development for Axalta is the November announcement of what is effectively a merger of equals with Akzo Nobel. Under the terms of the deal, Axalta shareholders will get 0.6539 Akzo shares for each of their shares, and Akzo shareholders will ultimately own 55% of the business (Akzo shareholders will also be getting a special dividend before the deal closes).

With the share prices having moved since the deal was announced, Akzo’s offer currently values Axalta at about 10.9x forward EBITDA. Although that is a sizable discount to the 13x EBITDA multiple that BASF (BASFY) got for its BASF Coatings business—a business that I would argue is no better than, and possibly inferior to, Axalta—it's not unfair relative to my estimate of a fair multiple based on Axalta’s likely margins and returns.

There are some positives to this deal, including a more diverse business mix and some potential revenue synergies (maybe 100 bp–200 bp) across the business. It also answers Akzo’s desire to diverse into auto markets and Axalta’s desire to diversify away from auto.

Nevertheless, I don’t like it as a long-term option for Axalta shareholders.

Akzo has been in a seemingly never-ending restructuring mode, and the shares are down 30% over the past five years and 20% over the past decade, with poor revenue, operating income, and free cash flow performance, as well as feeble metrics like ROIC.

In contrast, despite some weakness in this latest down-cycle, Axalta can at least boast better than 6% revenue growth over the last five years, market share gains, better free cash flow generation, and better ROICs. On a longer-term basis, Axalta has been gaining share in refinish and industrial and has done a good job of penetrating the Chinese auto market with a service-oriented model that locates within OEM facilities and offers a broader range of products than its main rivals.

In addition to my concerns about the quality of Akzo management, I have questions about the cost synergies. The target of $600 million is about 18% of combined EBITDA, which is on the high end of successful industrial mergers. I believe Axalta has improved its operations to a point where there isn’t a lot of low-hanging fruit now, and I question whether Akzo management is up to the challenge of achieving above-average cost synergies with an already efficiently run operation.

I have to acknowledge that Axalta has not done particularly well in the stock market over the last 10 years, but Akzo hasn’t done that much better. I might prefer this deal if it were Axalta management running the combined entity, but as is, I don’t see this as a clear long-term value builder for Axalta shareholders—there is upside, but that has to be discounted by the probability that management will not like it, and I have my doubts there.

The Outlook

Axalta offered weak guidance for the upcoming year, with EBITDA about 2% below the Street at the midpoint. I do see some possibilities for upside, though. Industrial markets are improving, and Axalta is leveraged to attractive sub-sectors like electrical products, and the comps will get easier from here. Likewise, I expect improving comps in the refinish market, and as miles driven continue to grow, accident frequency should increase.

I’m expecting around 2% revenue growth in 2026, improving to around 3% over the next three years and longer term. As end markets recover, I believe Axalta can slowly build EBITDA margins toward the mid-20%s, and I expect this to drive low double-digit free cash flow margins and solid mid-single-digit free cash flow growth over the long term.

Axalta looks undervalued on both a discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA approach. The former suggests a high single-digit long-term annualized potential return, while an 11x multiple on EBITDA would support a fair value near $47.50.

Valuation is now driven at least in part by the value of the pending Akzo merger. As much as I don’t like the deal, at this point the completion of the deal would offer meaningful upside, with an imputed value of over $46/share for Axalta.

The Bottom Line

I think Axalta is worth considering as long as there’s such a gap between Axalta’s share price and the imputed deal value. There are risks that the deal won’t go forward, but I think Axalta’s underlying standalone value offers some downside protection. I wouldn’t be all that interested in owning the new Akzo shares long term, but so long as the deal offers share price upside, this is a name worth considering, and particularly so given that end markets should start recovering as 2026 moves on.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: January CPI Report: Unusually Cooler Than Normal
data.description:
Originally published on February 13, 2026

By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

Consumer prices in January came in softer than expected, with the annual headline rate easing to 2.4%, from 2.7% previously. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, rose 2.5% year-over-year, slightly below last month’s reading. Though this month’s report is typically noisy amid seasonal effects, the lack of any upward surprise signals that price pressures remain contained.

While inflation remains above 2%, the report is reassuring for markets as it signals tariff pass-throughs have remained modest so far. The broader trend of cooling price pressures, especially as shelter inflation continues to normalize, should also ease concerns about renewed inflation momentum, suggesting the Federal Reserve’s 2% target is within reach.

Report details

  • Headline inflation rose 0.2% from the prior month, lower than expected, bringing the annual rate to 2.4%, from 2.7% prior. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, rose 0.3%, as expected, bringing the annual rate to 2.5%, the slowest pace since March 2021. While January inflation reports tend to be noisy, given that companies typically raise prices at the start of the year, this cycle’s tariff pass-through effects add an additional layer of complexity. Encouragingly, the lack of any real upward surprise suggests the trajectory of cooling price pressures remains intact.
  • The highly volatile energy category declined 1.5% in the month, weighed down by a significant drop in gasoline prices, which more than offset the rise in natural gas prices amid a cold snap in most parts of the country. This dynamic was similarly evident in the reversal in strength in restaurant prices, as colder weather likely curtailed dining out activity. Together with an easing in the pace of increase in grocery prices, overall food inflation appeared to normalize following last month’s spike.
  • Core inflation continues to be driven by services prices and shelter costs, which increased 0.2% in the period. Nevertheless, owners’ equivalent rent, a major component, has continued to steadily decelerate. Prices of discretionary services categories also rose, particularly airfares, which increased at the fastest pace since May 2022, suggesting robust demand for travel.
  • Tariff-sensitive goods categories showed limited upward pressure. The increase in apparel and household furnishings prices remained subdued. Similarly, the increase in vehicle prices was restrained, with used cars and truck prices declining meaningfully for the second month in a row. Meanwhile, electronics and personal care products did see price increases. Overall, while there may still be some lingering effects of tariff pass-through, barring additional new tariffs, this effect should begin to fade by mid-year as we pass the initial surge in tariff rates from a year ago.
  • The Fed's preferred supercore inflation measure increased by 0.6% in the period, keeping the annual rate flat at 2.7% from the prior month. The supercore inflation measure, which excludes shelter from core services and is primarily driven by wage costs, has not yet been an issue given the ongoing softening in labor demand.

Policy outlook

Overall, as inflation came in as expected, markets are likely breathing a sigh of relief as price pressures remain contained despite a very strong labor market report earlier in the week and amid persistent concerns over further tariff pass-through.

With core inflation now at almost a four-year low and the Fed’s 2% target within reach, this is a reassuring data point. Nevertheless, this falls short of justifying near-term rate cuts. Continued labor market resilience gives policymakers cover to stay on hold, while further disinflation in the second half of the year - as tariff effects fade - should reopen the door to further easing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: American Tower: Steadily Growing Long-Term Demand Drivers, Shares Attractive
data.description:
American Tower Corporation (AMT) is a real estate investment trust (“REIT”) that owns, operates, and develops multitenant communications real estate. This includes the tower structure, land parcel, and the backup power. In essence, their property holdings are critical components of the wireless communications networks used by millions across the U.S.

Shares in the stock are often one of the largest holdings by real estate ETFs offered by various brokerages. Within the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), for example, the stock is a top 5 holding, representing nearly 5% of the fund.

At present, the stock is trading around the mid-point of its 52-week range. For investors seeking an individualized play within the real estate sector, I believe AMT is one stock that should be an anchor holding. At current prices, I believe investors would be afforded a reasonable entry point into a durable company with a positive operational outlook.

AMT Stock Key Metrics

One aspect of AMT's business model is the long-term nature of the leases. Initial terms are generally 5 to 10 years, and embedded are multiple renewal terms. In addition, the leases include annual escalators averaging 3%. Tenant lock-in over this term is supplemented by relatively low capex requirements for AMT.

This business model enables AMT to generate a stable and recurring revenue stream, which in turn translates to consistent growth in EBITDA and adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”).

A case in point is that over the past 10 years from 2014 to 2024, AMT has grown segment revenue and adjusted EBITDA at a compound growth rate (“CAGR”) of about 10%. These ultimately have translated to significant overall shareholder returns, including a 17% CAGR in its dividend/share since 2014.

It shouldn't be a surprise, then, that Wall Street remains bullish overall on the stock, despite recent downgrades at the end of 2025 from Barclays. With regards to a price target, analysts covering the stock see shares fairly valued at around the $220/share mark. This would represent almost 15% upside potential from current trading values.

I believe AMT can reach this mark, especially given AMT’s favorable demand drivers. In its last investor presentation, AMT presented facts surrounding global mobile data usage over the next several years. The data was striking, in my view. In the U.S., usage is expected to grow about 12% CAGR from 2025 through 2030. In Europe, it's about 13% over the same period.

The continued double-digit percentage rise should continue to serve as leverage for AMT in both its lease rates and renewals as well as locking in new contracts. While AMT’s Q4 results are pending at the time of this article, Q3 results showed that AMT’s fundamentals and outlook remain positive, in my view.

AMT Operating Performance

AMT’s most recently completed quarter was Q3, and this was released at the end of October 2025. At that time, I viewed the results as an expectation beating quarter that was paired with positive forward guidance.

First, topline revenues during the period increased nearly 8% YOY, driven by steady mid-single-digit organic growth, strong U.S. services activity, and double-digit growth at CoreSite.

In addition, property revenue rose about 6%, while consolidated organic tenant billings growth came in at 5%. This is exactly the kind of steady, predictable performance investors expect from this business model.

More to the bottom line, adjusted EBITDA also climbed nearly 8%, supported not just by revenue growth but by disciplined cost management. In fact, cash margins expanded modestly, a continuing trend from past quarters.

The management team also highlighted roughly 300 basis points of EBITDA margin expansion since 2020. That operating leverage is important, particularly in a higher-rate environment, in my view.

Most notably, AFFO/share grew approximately 10% YOY. That’s strong bottom-line execution, especially for a company already operating at scale. Given these results, the guidance was confident enough to include a trifecta raise to full-year revenue, EBITDA, and AFFO.

Between the continued 5% organic tenant billings growth, 14%+ data center revenue growth, and leverage now below 5x, Q3 supported what I would view as the base case for AMT. That is durable demand and steady AFFO/share growth.

What Is The Outlook For AMT Stock?

As mentioned, Q4 included raised full-year guidance across revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and AFFO. At the midpoint, AMT now expects roughly 7% growth in attributable AFFO/share as adjusted, or closer to 9% when excluding FX and financing impacts.

Organic tenant billings growth is expected to remain steady at about 5%, with data center revenue expected to grow approximately 13% for the year. That is not hypergrowth, but it is consistent, visible, and supported by strong mobile data consumption trends globally, in my view.

AMT is set to report Q4 results pre-market on February 24, and based on the raised outlook and its YTD performance, I believe the company is well positioned to meet expectations.

Ahead of the release, AMT has faced some downgrades. At the end of December, for example, analysts at Barclays downgraded AMT to "equal weight," citing concerns around potential EchoStar-related rent collection risks and limited near-term re-rating potential.

The price target was also trimmed to $200/share. While that headline created some noise, the broader analyst community still sees fair value closer to $220, implying even greater upside from current levels.

In my view, the downgrade reflects caution around a specific tenant situation rather than deterioration in AMT’s core operating trends. I ultimately believe the long-term demand drivers remain firmly intact.

Is AMT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I like AMT at current levels, and I see it as a Buy for investors seeking positioning in the real estate sector.

The stock is trading around the midpoint of its 52-week range, even as the company continues to deliver mid-to-high single-digit organic growth and double-digit AFFO/share growth. Given the results, I believe investors are today being offered a reasonable entry point into a business with long-term, visible demand drivers that are not cyclical in nature.

In terms of demand-side factors, mobile data usage continues to grow at a double-digit pace globally. In the U.S. alone, consumption has been increasing at an extraordinary rate, and management expects usage to continue compounding through the end of the decade.

More data usage ultimately requires more network capacity, more densification, and more equipment on towers. That directly benefits AMT’s leasing activity and renewal economics. Add to that the continued momentum in CoreSite and early AI-related workloads, and the runway appears long, in my view.

Yes, there are near-term headlines around tenant disputes and downgrades. But I believe the core operating engine, positive organic tenant billings growth, expanding margins, and disciplined capital allocation, remains intact.

For investors seeking a durable REIT with steady AFFO growth and a long-term infrastructure tailwind, I believe AMT deserves to be an anchor holding at today’s price.

data.title: Nvidia And The Architecture Of The Technological Dollar
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), which is up only about 3% since my last analysis in December 2025, might look stuck in consolidation. However, the company is emerging as the primary provider of the structural architecture behind a latent dollar restrengthening regime. Ultimately, this reemergence of structural unipolarity will be driven by autonomous technologies driving higher GDP growth. Of course, this cannot happen without Nvidia.

This technological age will drive Nvidia's primacy in three ways. As AI productivity diffuses into core workflows, financial rails become more programmable, and USD network effects reconsolidate dominant-currency pricing and reserve status, Nvidia will likely be as ubiquitous as water. The cycle of growth we are already witnessing the birth of is as follows:

Compute > AI Diffusion > Productivity > GDP Growth > Fiscal Optics > USD Asset Demand

It's very simple, and it is surprising that sovereign states have been so quick to attack the world order in a temporary pocket of dollar weakness. Nvidia monetizes the conversion of labor and software spend into intelligent compute, and when that conversion becomes economy-wide and deeply penetrated, it will raise potential growth and improve debt-to-GDP via the denominator. Of course, debt reduction remains policy-driven, but consensus is so strong on the point of debt reduction necessity right now that the next decade looks like a clear path to victory.

Perhaps the greatest structural risk is not the debt-to-GDP itself, but the fear of geopolitical crisis in the interim driving irrational acts of war that could delay or destabilize markets in the interim. Patience, restraint, and technological advance appear to be all that is necessary for stability in the world order and its markets to return.

New-Era Financial Architecture

USD tokenization, or the so-called "digital dollar," has a high chance of underpinning a next-generation monetary and financial system. While this may presently seem distant, truthfully it is already being implemented, as evidenced by the Bank for International Settlements. Scalable tokenized rails across central bank reserves, commercial bank money, and government bonds imply heavier real-time advanced compute needs, likely augmented by agentic AI, which Nvidia will be the primary structural base of.

If AI more broadly increases the GDP growth of America first, and its allies second, and its direct competitors third, then USD invoicing and pricing can persist as a stable equilibrium in world trade. At that juncture, upgrades in U.S.-based financial plumbing through mechanisms such as tokenization will likely disproportionately reinforce demand for dollar-denominated collateral and settlement risks.

Bear in mind that we are already seeing structural evidence of this thesis playing out, so it is not wishful thinking or speculation. Customer service deployments show about a 14% productivity lift from genAI assistance, coding field experiments show more than 50% higher code output for junior staff, and controlled Copilot experiments are showing about a 55.8% faster task completion. Of course, higher efficiency equals higher demand for the source of such efficiency gains, which at the fundamental level of raw compute is Nvidia. Anecdotally, I am hearing reports and seeing evidence of people completing 4x the amount of work they used to without AI, at the high end of the curve for advanced AI adoption. I expect eventually this will become the norm.

Overcoming Constraints

In the near term, power and infrastructure will be more constrained than chip supply. The IEA estimates that around 20% of data center projects could be at risk of delays absent grid fixes, and it notes that transmission buildouts can take between four and eight years. Nvidia's own 10-Q warns that customers may delay adopting new architectures if their data center infrastructure isn't ready, so even if demand is strong due to accruing efficiency benefits across the macroeconomy from AI adoption, Nvidia's near-term revenue slope may be capped and volatility may emerge temporarily as a result. I would see this as a buying opportunity for NVDA stock, as I forecast expectations will then normalize, energy access will be innovated on and strengthened across the free world, and Nvidia will have a following bull market, "phase 2," in the AI supercycle.

The mitigation lever to this energy constraint that Nvidia has control over is its deliverance of performance per watt. Nvidia has cited SemiAnalysis InferenceMAX benchmarks where already Blackwell has delivered 10x throughput per megawatt versus the prior generation. That said, there is clearly an ongoing opportunity here for refined chip architectures, and it's clear why Nvidia recently licensed Groq's inference tech and hired key Groq personnel.

Geopolitical Risks

Multipolar war arriving early is a potential medium-term outcome that could materially fragment the trajectory of AI productivity diffusing into the Western economies and improving U.S. fiscal optics. For Nvidia, among most other tech companies, the Taiwan risk is existential for near-term cash flows. Nvidia is dependent on TSMC (TSM) in Taiwan to manufacture its semiconductors. CSIS estimates about $2.45T of goods, over one-fifth of global maritime trade, transited the Taiwan Strait. Therefore, disruption becomes a trade, commodity, and financing shock that could probably freeze the data center buildout cycle. Albeit, this would be a Chinese kamikaze.

Nonetheless, GMFUS states a major war over Taiwan would likely separate a smaller global economy into three blocs, which would force NVDA growth forecasts to be bloc-constrained. At least temporarily, technological dollar stabilization would become a regional outcome rather than a global one. Yet meanwhile, China would end up as an isolated, heavily sanctioned state in a degenerated multipolar world order that likely would not endure.

At present, BIS research on international debt securities finds that the dollar share in 2024 is roughly equivalent to its share in 2000, while the world lacks a viable replacement. This clearly supports the continued depth of U.S. capital markets while AI infrastructure is being financed. China may have renminbi-denominated transaction networks and cross-border payment projects that it is designing with Western sanctions in mind, but this will never realistically be a global financial anchor in the 21st century and rather acts as a geoeconomic backstop for the CCP.

Valuation

Firstly, we must address the elephant in the room. The S&P 500 is de facto overvalued at the time of this writing without AI-driven narrative sentiment holding up a presently negative earnings yield spread. Albeit, such narrative sustenance is possible, because if the margin and GDP growth benefits are as strong as the emerging Nvidia-anchored autonomous economy suggests, the S&P 500 may in fact actually be cheap. That said, it is far too risky to bet the farm on a structural revolution in stock market valuation mechanics. To be clear, I am not saying that won't happen, though.

Nvidia specifically has a strong few years of consensus growth ahead of it, but I am more concerned about the calendar year 2028, which is Nvidia's fiscal 2029. My current premonition, based on data findings from the energy sector discussed above, is that capex spending may curtail temporarily due to bottlenecks in calendar year 2028. As markets price about a year in advance, 2027 appears as a plausible correction window. I cannot be sure of this, but it appears logical given the present valuation excess, the reality of finite Earth-bound energy, and the compressing and even negative free cash flow emerging amongst hyperscalers.

As a result, my current base-case forecast looks as follows. I find it plausible that NVDA will test all-time highs slightly and moderately by the end of calendar year 2026, before a meaningful correction and buying window emerges before phase 2 of the AI supercycle begins.

Conclusion: Hold

Those who believe that multipolarity will define geopolitics over the next turn of history may be in for an awakening. The Nvidia-anchored autonomous world order is already emerging, but it has not yet bloomed. This spud, tended to carefully by an elite who are focused on energy and intelligence and not concerned with the binary spoils and travesties of war, has the potential to revitalize the dollar in a new technological form and age. There is little reason to be bearish on Nvidia long-term, but instead, one has to place the stock price in perspective with broader macro valuations and real energy constraints to position capital with prudence, foresight, and respect for the global order at hand.

Addendum: Q4 Preview

Nvidia reports Q4 FY26 results on Wednesday, Feb 25, after the market closes. I would be particularly interested to hear CEO Jensen Huang's comments on what his outlook is on the energy environment, though I expect his wording to be conservative given how impactful his statements are on stock sentiment.

I understand how bullish Huang and the company are on the long-term hyperscaling trajectory, but I am hoping for comments in the earnings call about the fiscal condition of its major customers and the reliability of compounding growth for NVDA in the medium term in the face of emerging compressing and even negative free cash flow for these customers. Ultimately, we can expect this earnings print to be positive, and I have a feeling that management will not let any near-term weaknesses leak, instead focusing on the more positive, optimistic, and bullish long horizon of intelligent compute dominance that Nvidia commands.

data.title: Microsoft's Azure Reacceleration Could Shock Wall Street
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), despite being on the edge of AI development and having multiple layers of future monetization from this novel tech, has sold off the most since the beginning of 2026, and its negative momentum "beats" Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), Nvidia (NVDA), and actually all top-7 tech firms, let alone the S&P 500 index.

The market seems to be punishing MSFT for the lack of immediate return on invested capital. CAPEX is rising, but they aren't converting a sufficient share of M365 commercial seats into paid Copilot users, which is one of the reasons the market is concerned about the firm's future standing in the AI race. But I think it's premature to judge MSFT's future success based solely on a few metrics. The catalysts that I see ahead seem to give MSFT a comfortable upside potential, in my opinion, so the YTD selloff has given long-term investors a nice opportunity to load up while the stock is relatively cheap.

Catalysts 2026-2028: Agents, Silicon, And Quantum Supremacy

The main catalyst that I see for Microsoft is its transition toward agentic AI and the computing infrastructure around of it. Today's chatbots are mainly used for data collection and representation, and oftentimes, when one tries to run a reasoning-based research task, these bots fail to perform at a sufficient quality. However, the current level of AI development can already make agents work at a good level - agents can manage workflows (which doesn't require much "comprehension" from them) or perform standard coding tasks. According to Grand View Research's study, the AI agents market is expected to grow from $7.63 billion (as of 2025) up to $183 billion by 2033. And Microsoft seems to be one of the potential winners of this market due to things like the OpenClaw open-source platform. Here's how another Seeking Alpha author, Gene Chan, CFA, described this product's capabilities in his article:

You interact with OpenClaw just like you could with a human assistant—converse with it via chat apps on your phone, such as WhatsApp or Telegram.

You describe in plain English (or another human language) what you want, when you want it, and how you want it.

The power of OpenClaw comes from the fact that it is open-sourced, can be hosted on the cloud or locally, and can basically connect to any services you give it access to:

Your Google Mail, Amazon account, travel agent, bank account, brokerage, and Seeking Alpha account... you get the idea.

This magical combination of persistence and flexibility layered on top of the latest LLM for core intelligence made OpenClaw the first autonomous agent to really live up to its role.

(Taken from Gene Chan, CFA's article)

Gene Chan wrote that Microsoft can basically evolve Copilot into an "Enterprise OpenClaw" by granting agents unique identities in the enterprise LDAP (similar to human employees). And it can then provide them with human-like access to corporate environments managed via established Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions. It's unlikely the area of the market's interest right now; otherwise, the stock wouldn't have tanked on higher CAPEX projections. But if we recall the level of so-called "under-utilization" of MSFT's 450 million-seat M365 commercial base, and if we assume that this agentic tech can increase conversions thanks to its cost-effectiveness, I think MSFT's AI opportunity has only become bigger over the past few months.

Another catalyst that makes MSFT a better investment now compared to, say, 2024, is the firm's progress on hardware stack verticalization. In other words, Microsoft has launched the Maia 200 AI accelerator, which was built on TSMC's (TSM) 3nm process with 100 billion transistors, and this accelerator should have 3-4x performance of Amazon's Trainium3 chip (and outpace Google's (GOOG) TPU v7 as well).

If this silicon can save up to 30% in per-dollar performance for Microsoft (vs. GPUs), the company decouples its inference costs from Nvidia's (NVDA) pricing power, which is a great feature if we keep in mind that inference workloads are going to outweigh training over time (in some occurrences, it's already happening).

Microsoft also introduced the Cobalt 200 CPU that offers up to 50% "higher performance compared to their first custom-built processor for cloud-native workloads", per the management's commentaries on the earnings call. It's very important for Azure's profitability over time.

By the way, Azure (with other cloud services revenues combined) went up by 39% YoY in Q2, which was only 1% lower vs. Q1. The platform is capacity-constrained, based on commentaries, so it basically means that once Microsoft adds additional capacity and it optimizes performance with the above-discussed tech stack, Azure is likely to surprise the market to the upside, in my opinion.

Another catalyst that the market seems to ignore for now comes from the Majorana 1 chip that Microsoft introduced last year, which can lead to the industrialization of quantum computing. They're using breakthrough topoconductor materials to create qubits (the basic unit of information used to encode data in quantum computing, per IBM's (IBM) definition) that are digitally controlled via voltage pulses. We can say that with Majorana, MSFT can scale to 1 million qubits "on a single chip that fits in the palm of a hand", as Andy Pardoe put it in his article.

Unlike what NVDA's CEO, Jensen Huang, said about quantum computing tech's development timeline, MSFT's representatives noted that their QC will be within years (not decades). If so, Microsoft can industrialize quantum computing ahead of others and open up a completely new TAM layer for itself over the next few years, in my understanding.

Triggers For MSFT's Bullish Repricing

First, I expect Azure revenue to beat after-Q2 expectations that were likely hammered after the earnings release. Seeking Alpha data clearly shows that over the past 30 days, the analysts' revenue consensus slightly dropped for the upcoming 2 quarters.

The firm is going to spend more on cloud and AI needs, and we've seen how quickly Azure can grow the top line in fiscal 2026 - with more spending ahead, I think Azure's growth will only accelerate. Especially so as MSFT keeps adding supply capacity (from massive new sites like Fairwater). So, there's a high chance that Azure will come back to 40%+ growth in Q3-Q4, and if it does, the company should beat the consolidated revenue growth consensus.

Second, the restructuring of OpenAI liability can become a relief factor if OpenAI renegotiates its commitments or diversifies its infrastructure to other clouds. It sounds a bit counterintuitive - why would that decoupling be a relief for MSFT? But the market seems to be viewing the $250 billion commitment from OpenAI as a concentration risk, so ironically, this kind of decoupling should be accretive to Microsoft, allowing it to step off the low-margin compute recycling and reallocate those funds into high-ROIC ventures like Copilot and agentic services that I described above.

Third, we don't have to wait for the next quarterly release to see a potential bounce back above $500 per share because there's a technical analysis setup forming. The MACD levels are in the "buy" zone, and the RSI is the lowest it's been since April 2025, when MSFT dropped and then strongly rebounded. The odds for another leg upward look quite high to me.

Fourth, MSFT's valuation levels are also supportive for an eventual recovery. When the stock was trading this cheap on a P/E basis over the past few years, investors then saw a strong move to the upside:

MSFT is the 2nd cheapest company in the top-7 group based on the forward GAAP P/E ratio, and it's the 3rd cheapest on the forward EV/EBITDA basis, according to Seeking Alpha.

I think there will be bullish revisions ahead for Microsoft's EPS annual figures - assuming 5% on top of FY2027 EPS projections doesn't look like something unrealistic to me. So, instead of the current FY2027 $18.85 EPS, I get ~$19.79/share in earnings for the year. After putting 25-27x on that corrected EPS estimation, I get a price target of $514.6 per share, which is ~28% above today's stock price. And that PT of mine looks very conservative when we compare it to what Wall Street's consensus predicts:

Risks To My Views

While I noted multiple times throughout the article that I believe MSFT's CAPEX should lead to better Azure growth ahead, the company is investing quite aggressively now, and there's a major risk that we won't see any long-term value from those investments. The management noted that 2/3 of the current spend is directed toward GPUs and CPUs, which have a relatively short-lived nature - if MSFT faces physical and functional obsolescence of AI hardware sooner than I expect, the firm's margins will drop. And if Copilot subscription growth doesn't scale fast enough, there will be risks to the top line, and I believe it will only make the situation worse.

Also, MSFT may not be as undervalued as I view it. The stock might keep dropping lower, hitting 18-20x forward P/E at some point. If you recall, when Google was on sale in early 2025, its GAAP P/E was reaching 17x at one point. So, if we assume that MSFT follows a similar price action path, we might see the stock touching low-$300/share at some point.

Conclusion

From what I see, MSFT is doing everything right - without CAPEX increases, they could lose the AI race to others, so it's a necessity. So far, the market's concerns regarding the lack of ROIC look overblown to me. There are plenty of monetization levers ahead for Microsoft, in my opinion, that are currently ignored by the market. The likely combination of a slight multiple expansion with a single round of positive forward EPS consensus adjustment points to an undervaluation of about 28%. Wall Streeters have an average price target at 48%+, so MSFT is starting to look like a steal after its YTD selloff.

data.title: Pfizer: The Rebound Looks Unsustainable
data.description:
I remain cautious regarding Pfizer Inc. (PFE) despite the stock having earned an impressive 17% total return for its investors since early November, when I shared a neutral opinion. I am not buying this stock and do not give a bullish recommendation because the rally since early November appears to be explained not by PFE's fundamentals but by the overall rotation from growth to value as "AI bubble" fears mount.

If we delve into PFE's fundamentals, we can see that there were no big company-specific developments justifying a massive rally since early November. Wall Street analysts expect PFE's P/E ratio to expand over the next few years as the massive adverse effect of the patent cliff effect is expected to kick in starting in 2026. Therefore, the share price rebound does not look sustainable, as it does not stem from company-specific factors. Therefore, I remain skeptical and reiterate a Hold rating for PFE.

Fundamental Analysis

Pfizer's share price is up by almost 11% year-to-date, which is by far better share price action compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and the entire Healthcare sector's (XLV) performance. I think that this rally is explained by the broader market's sentiment with growing concerns around the "AI bubble" rather than Pfizer's company-specific factors. I arrived at this conclusion based on the several key developments surrounding Pfizer's fundamentals.

The company released its Q4 and full-year earnings a couple of weeks ago. There were positive surprises both from the revenue and EPS perspectives, which is a positive factor. Delivering double earnings beats is always good in terms of fortifying investors' trust in your company. Apart from the fact that there was a dual beat, the rest did not look that good. Pfizer's revenue stagnation continued in Q4 with a 1% topline decline on a year-over-year basis. Pfizer's quarterly EPS increased slightly compared to the same quarter of 2024, from $0.63 to $0.66. However, if we look at it on a sequential basis, Pfizer's Q4 2025 EPS was the lowest compared to each of the previous three quarters, while its Q4 revenue was the highest among all quarters of 2025. Such convergence does not add optimism, as it indicates the fact that the company struggles to exercise operating leverage.

Finally, the FY2026 guidance was weaker than consensus expectations. It indicates that the management still struggles to find ways to mitigate the fundamental patent cliff problem, which I described in deep detail in my previous PFE articles.

To summarize, the problem is not only Pfizer's past financial performance but is also in a very cloudy outlook. The midpoint of the FY2026 revenue range guidance is $61 billion, which will be approximately $1.5 billion lower compared to FY2025 revenue and almost $2.6 billion lower compared to FY2024. The third year in a row with revenue decline looks like a huge red flag, especially given Pfizer's EPS instability during FY2025. The midpoint of the EPS guidance range is $2.9, which will also be lower than FY2025's $3.23 per share. I also want to draw readers' attention to the fact that Pfizer's EPS is expected to decline faster than revenue, a projected 10% EPS decrease compared to a 2.4% revenue decrease. It implies that even though the management achieved cost savings of a few billion dollars over the last several quarters, it still does not mitigate the adverse effect of revenue stagnation.

With EPS poised to decline further, it will be extremely difficult for PFE to maintain its FCF-generating capacity. With deteriorating cash-generating capacity, it will be challenging to deleverage Pfizer's current, quite aggressive total debt of more than $60 billion. Therefore, the company's financial flexibility is also poised to weaken if the deteriorating EPS trend continues. To mitigate the patent cliff risk, the company needs to maintain aggressive R&D levels. With the likely shrinking FCF generating capacity and quite aggressive leverage, the management might be forced to sacrifice either R&D, CapEx, or dividend growth.

Finally, the simple moving averages ("SMAs") setup speaks bearish as well. PFE currently trades with an 8.5% premium compared to its 100-day SMA, which doesn't look sustainable if we look at how the share price performed historically after it climbed to its 100-day SMA. The same logic applies to the 50-day SMA. Moreover, the stock is approaching an "overbought" territory as its 14-day RSI is currently above 60.

To sum up, a combination of fundamental and technical indicators suggests that PFE's rally is poised to finish very soon.

Valuation Analysis

While I always seek multiple compressions in stocks, PFE is the exact opposite. Its forward P/E ratio setup is bearish as Wall Street analysts expect the company's multiple to increase from 9 today to 13 by the end of FY2029. A quite aggressive P/E ratio expansion is expected due to high patent cliff risks, which I described in detail in my previous PFE coverages.

Of course, if we ignore forward P/E and look at the current multiples, the picture looks better. However, investors pay for future profits and cash flows, and with the expected EPS decline, PFE's current low multiples compared to historical averages and to the sector median look absolutely justified.

The fact that Pfizer's low current multiples do not mean significant undervaluation is also confirmed by the average target price from Wall Street analysts of $28.6. This is just above the last close, meaning that analysts see almost no upside potential left on the table.

Mitigating Factors

While at the moment Wall Street analysts forecast EPS decline in 2026-2029, which is a clear bearish sign, it doesn't mean that the forward P/E trajectory will never change. For an innovative pharmaceutical giant like PFE, new blockbuster products mean a lot in terms of their long-term EPS growth potential. The company has a big fundamental patent cliff issue, but given PFE's massive $10+ billion R&D budget and an extensive pipeline with more than 100 candidate products, the situation can change. A potential new blockbuster product can have a notable positive effect on the company's long-term EPS outlook, which will likely trigger a rally.

If the AI bubble narrative continues to become stronger and more frequently mentioned by reputable leaders and experts, investors might continue rotating to value aggressively. While there are questions regarding PFE's capital appreciation potential due to the expected EPS decline over the next few years, it is impossible to argue that Pfizer is a dividend king that has been paying dividends over the last several decades. The current forward yield is still attractive at 6.2%, even after the share price increase of the last few months. The dividend growth rate over the last five years outpaced inflation, which is likely to be another attractive moment for investors seeking balance between dividend yield and growth. If investors continue rotating to dividend kings like PFE, it will inevitably push the share price up further regardless of EPS growth potential over the last few years because the stock market is a voting machine over the short term.

Conclusion

The recent share price rebound demonstrated by PFE does not look sustainable because there are no big company-specific bullish catalysts on the horizon. The company's EPS is poised to decline due to the approaching negative patent cliff catalyst, and the partial EPS rebound is only possible if PFE releases new blockbuster products, which is highly uncertain. Therefore, I reiterate a Hold rating for PFE.

data.title: Adobe: The SaaS Apocalypse Is About To Meet A Valuation Check
data.description:
Adobe Inc. (ADBE) was once a market darling. Now, the company is widely viewed to be a potential victim of generative AI. That perceived threat, however, has not yet made itself shown in the numbers, with the company still delivering double-digit top-line growth, attractive profit margins, and aggressive share repurchases. Moreover, I am increasingly growing of the view that the company may instead prove to be a potential beneficiary of generative AI as the fears look misplaced. The stock is trading too cheaply given the strong balance sheet. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

ADBE Stock Price

I last covered ADBE in November, where I rated the stock a "Strong Buy" due to the aggressive share buyback program. The stock has crumbled 22% since.

This may be the most controversial time in the company’s history, but management is showing that it isn’t going down without a fight.

ADBE Stock Key Metrics

The enterprise software company looks to be squarely in generative AI’s crosshairs. Generative AI chatbots can generate beautiful images from scratch, which on the surface presents long-term threats to ADBE’s business. But lingering in the corners is the potential pushback that all these generated images may need professional-level editing to modify and maintain. Only time will tell which narrative proves correct, but meanwhile the company has been generating solid numbers. In the most recent quarter, the company generated 10% YoY revenue growth to $6.19 billion, destroying guidance of $6.075 billion to $6.125 billion. Non-GAAP earnings of $5.50 per share also exceeded guidance of between $5.35 and $5.40 per share.

Ironically, the company saw its strongest growth from its “digital media segment,” which houses the supposed products in the crosshairs of generative AI, including Photoshop and Illustrator. The company also repurchased 7.2 million shares in the quarter, and it has generally directed all free cash flow to share repurchases.

Looking ahead, management guided the first quarter to see up to $6.3 billion in revenue, representing 10.5% YoY growth.

For the full year, management guided for up to $26.1 billion in revenue, representing 9.7% YoY growth. Given that this is a management team that typically underperforms and overdelivers, I would not be surprised if the company delivers double-digit top-line growth yet again.

In the prepared remarks, management attempted to highlight their growing AI adoption, noting that “credit consumption increased 3x quarter over quarter.” Management noted that subscribers who use more AI products “have the choice of moving to higher-value Creative Cloud offerings or acquiring Firefly Credit Add-ons.” Management noted that their acquisition of Semrush is due to their observation that “AI-powered traffic from LLMs and agentic browsers is rising.” I view that acquisition as management using their balance sheet to increase their relevance in the AI age.

On the conference call, management noted that AI products like Firefly are adding incremental revenues, noting one customer who was previously spending $10 million has since added “Firefly Services and Firefly Foundry for about $7 million.” Management believes that AI has made their products more valuable, increasing the “pricing opportunity.”

Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, ADBE traded at just 11x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to post minimal operating leverage despite projections for high-single-digit top-line growth.

At this point, I do not think it is prudent to continue hoping for a pronounced acceleration and top-line growth. The main bullish thesis here is that of a re-rating based on sentiment. It is difficult to pinpoint exactly what and when this catalyst might occur. That said, I like what I see. Management remains focused on profitable growth all while executing on an aggressive share repurchase program. Backed by a strong balance sheet, I find it unlikely for the stock to continue trading at these levels for long - even acknowledging the negative sentiment. I expect the product to remain sticky for the vast majority of large corporations. Where I detract with broader market fears is that I do not expect generative AI to disrupt the company (or at least not by that much). Instead, I expect ADBE (like many other software companies) to find success in integrating generative AI into their products. This would both increase the ease of use and the functionality and capabilities of the products. Yes, generative AI currently allows image generation, but these images need to be maintained and edited. ADBE might face near-term headwinds from its smaller consumer business (the mix is not disclosed), but I expect its more important enterprise business to more or less be unaffected by generative AI. I note that I am not assigning any potential upside from the company's integration within OpenAI. The main thing to note here is that due to there not being any obvious catalyst on the horizon, the upside might be capped. I find it highly unlikely that this name returns to its former glory at 35x to 40x earnings. Instead, I am targeting a fair value range of 16x to 23x earnings. Due to the strong balance sheet, high GAAP margins, and share repurchases, I could see the stock reaching the low end of that range even assuming that growth rates fall to the low-single-digit range. That suggests ample upside potential over the coming years.

ADBE Stock Risks

ADBE might be getting disrupted. It is possible that the growth that it has been showing in recent quarters is temporary and may disappear as disruption emerges. Profit margins might deteriorate instead of expand if the company loses pricing power. Management might engage in aggressive M&A in attempts to fix the narrative. This might lead to shareholder dilution as well as damage to the balance sheet. It is possible that revenue growth not only slows but also turns negative in bearish scenarios.

ADBE Stock Conclusion

I like what I am seeing from the company, despite the negative broader sentiment. The strong balance sheet means that management can continue to make strategic acquisitions to fortify the product portfolio. With the stock trading at cheap valuations, investors should take relief that the company is repurchasing shares so aggressively. ADBE is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: JEPQ: Collect A 10.5% Yield While Hedging Your Bets
data.description:
The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) is one of the most popular covered call funds in the world. With $33.57 billion in assets under management (AUM), the fund is among JPMorgan Asset Management’s biggest.

Owing to the volatility of its underlying portfolio, JEPQ has a very high dividend yield. The fund’s yield is 10.67%, compared to 8% for its sister fund, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI). Despite the similarities between the NASDAQ 100 and the S&P 500, such as them having nearly the same top five holdings, the former index is just slightly more volatile than the latter. Options increase in value with the volatility of their underlying holdings, as a more volatile asset has a higher return while in the money but an unaffected return while out of the money (out-of-the-money options always pay their owners negative 100% before premiums if they hold to expiration). As a result, JEPQ is able to pay out a lot of income to unit holders.

One major criticism of covered call funds like JEPQ stems from the fact that they cap upside. When you sell covered calls, you agree to sell your shares at an agreed-upon price (the strike price). Very frequently, the underlying shares go much higher than the strike price, meaning that you as the seller miss out on considerable upside.

This criticism is true in normal market conditions. If stocks are going up by high percentages, then covered calls do hurt total returns. However, there are two major caveats here:

  1. Covered call writing maximizes returns in sideways markets, as well as in bull markets where the trend is too small for the strike price to be hit.

  2. Covered call writing also smooths out volatility by providing an income buffer and periodically selling off portions of the portfolio. This can be empirically demonstrated in JEPQ’s case: the fund has a five-year monthly beta coefficient of 0.78, which means that the fund’s average move is only 78% of its benchmark's average move.

Of the two points above, the first is probably the most substantial. There are certain market conditions under which the covered call strategy is optimal, namely, conditions with either no trend or a very weak trend.

Let’s say you have a portfolio of NASDAQ 100 stocks that you are seeking to generate income from. The NASDAQ 100 yields just 0.46%, so you’ll need some yield enhancement to achieve your goal. You decide that covered call writing is a good way to get some income. So, you write calls, secured with the stocks in your portfolio. You set the strike price 10% above the current index level and the expiration date as December 31. You get a premium worth 5% of the underlying portfolio value. The stocks rise 8% over the course of the year, meaning that the strike price is not hit. In this scenario, you enjoy a 13.46% return (5% premium income plus 8% capital gains plus a 0.46% dividend yield) from a basket of stocks that only rose 8%. Not bad.

Today, we are witnessing market conditions quite similar to those under which covered calls perform best. The markets have been trading roughly sideways since the beginning of the year, and there are some signs that this lack of a clear trend will persist for the remainder of the year at least. For this reason, I am maintaining my JEPQ rating at buy, a rating I will elaborate on in the ensuing paragraphs.

My Thesis Confirmed

Before getting into my reasons for maintaining my JEPQ rating at buy, I should explore the results obtained from my last article on the fund. The rating was buy, and those who bought the fund at the time I rated it would have been outperforming the market as of the market close on Friday, February 13.

Covered calls are notorious for capping upside, the reason being that they can force you to sell your shares. Given this, it might look odd that JEPQ has been outperforming the broader U.S. market of late. Isn’t it a handicap to have your portfolio upside capped?

Not necessarily. As I explained previously, covered call yield enhancement boosts returns in sideways markets and in ‘weak’ bull markets where the strike price on OTM puts isn’t hit. Additionally, covered calls provide a partial hedge against downside in bear markets. So, there are many conditions where covered calls actually increase your return. In the next section, I’ll explain why I expect these conditions to persist for the remainder of the year.

AI and the Markets: A Complex Picture

In the introduction, I wrote that I expected the markets (specifically the NASDAQ 100 index) to trade sideways or close to sideways for the remainder of the year. This is a very specific forecast, one that likely isn’t obvious to most people. So, I should explain it in detail.

The NASDAQ 100 index is presently home to most of America’s large publicly traded AI companies. Alphabet (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Oracle (ORCL) are all components of the NASDAQ 100. These companies are known for rapid growth, wide margins, and strong competitive positions. Due to their heavy investments in advanced technologies, these companies rapidly acquired dominant positions in various segments of the economy. Also, for most of their histories, they did not compete especially strongly with one another, instead focusing on different parts of the tech world. Google always focused on information retrieval, Meta always focused on social interaction, and Amazon always focused on retail. This differentiation helped all players.

In November 2022, things started to change for America’s big tech companies. That month, OpenAI launched its popular chatbot ChatGPT on X (formerly known as Twitter), in what rapidly became the most successful tech launch of all time to that point. The app’s AI capabilities wowed users and kicked off a flurry of investment in AI that swept the entire industry. Within a few months of ChatGPT’s release, other tech companies were trying to ride OpenAI’s coattails, launching chatbots of their own. By early 2023, Alphabet and Microsoft had fully functional chatbots; somewhat later, they were followed by companies like xAI (X.AI), Alibaba (BABA), and Baidu (BIDU).

The chatbots offered by big tech companies are all fairly similar. The UI shows a text box that you can use to ask questions or request things, such as essays, articles, images, and videos. The AI companies are not currently profitable on their generative AI investments and do not expect to be for some time. These unprofitable investments are also quite large, with $700 billion in CAPEX expected for 2026. As a result, big tech companies are now taking on large amounts of debt and seeing negative free cash flow (FCF) growth. Put simply, it’s an intensely competitive environment, quite different from the environment in which the big tech giants first established their fast-growing, wide-margin ways. If these unprofitable AI bets continue growing forever, then some of the biggest QQQ component stocks are in trouble.

Do I mean to say that the entire NASDAQ 100 index is in massive trouble here?

No. For one thing, at least one of the big tech companies, NVIDIA, profits from the CAPEX that all the other AI companies are undertaking: it sells the GPUs that those companies use in their data centers. Second, other tech companies, such as Google, Apple (AAPL), and Amazon (AMZN), are working on in-house AI chip development. Those companies should enjoy higher margins in the future should they succeed. Nevertheless, the fact remains that competition between America’s big tech companies has gotten more intense. That should result in more volatility and lower total returns going forward.

Why JEPQ Could Shine Here

The reason why JEPQ could really shine in this market is because the underlying index is in a period of elevated risk, risk that the fund is partially hedged against. Any well-diversified portfolio should have exposure to U.S. big tech, as that’s one of the biggest pools of wealth on the planet. To 'own the market,' you must own big tech. However, these stocks are undeniably risky. They are near all-time highs. They trade at high P/E ratios. They are facing elevated competitive pressure due to generative AI. The story here is much more complex than it was in the old days when the ‘FAANG’/‘Mag Seven’ stocks all did their own thing and competed little with one another. Today, they are locked into intense head-to-head competition in AI model development, chatbots, and cloud services—those are uncharted waters for the group.

So, to go long on these stocks via JEPQ could be wiser than to do an unhedged long bet, as with the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). The option premiums provide a nice buffer against downside (about 10%); the capping of upside is less of a concern now, at a time of elevated risk. Managing risk takes precedence over seeking high returns here, as it’s not clear that big tech stocks are going to deliver high returns going forward.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on JEPQ is that it’s one of the best ways to play the NASDAQ 100 right now, in my opinion. With its yield enhancement and partial hedging, it is one of the few funds available to retail investors that includes built-in risk management. In this environment of elevated risk and intensified competition, that really makes a difference.

data.title: VPLS: Another Look At This Solid, Vanilla Vanguard Bond ETF
data.description:
The Vanguard Core-Plus Bond ETF (VPLS) is an actively managed ETF focusing on simple investment-grade bonds, treasuries, and MBS, with smaller investments in foreign and high-yield bonds. VPLS is a simple, strong, vanilla bond ETF with a well-balanced portfolio, an above-average 4.5% dividend yield, and consistent outperformance since inception, and is a buy. (Our last article on it was here).

VPLS: Overview And Analysis

Strategy And Portfolio

VPLS is an actively managed bond ETF by Vanguard. Although the company is more well-known for its index ETFs, it does have a couple active offerings, and that includes VPLS.

The fund's active management could lead to higher income and returns moving forward, contingent on its managers making effective decisions. Active management is something of a risk factor too, as managers might make ineffective, unprofitable decisions too. Active ETFs have the highest potential returns, but index ETFs are generally safer, so focusing on these is always a reasonable choice.

Although VPLS is active, it is quite similar to its benchmark or index, the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND). There are some important differences, but the broad strokes are the same.

VPLS focuses on high-quality, investment-grade securities, with treasuries and MBS accounting for just under half of its portfolio. Most of its other half consists of quality investment-grade corporate bonds, with smaller, single-digit investments in high-yield bonds. Allocations are as follows:

Diversification is quite good, due to the above and due to the fund investing in over 2,000 different bonds. BND invests in over 10,000, which increases diversification somewhat, although at these levels the impact is marginal: thousands of securities are more than enough to ensure a diversified portfolio. At the same time, VPLS's portfolio is a bit broader than that of BND, broader than most other bond ETFs too, which means higher diversification too.

Risk And Volatility

As mentioned previously, VPLS focuses on high-quality securities, with smaller investments in riskier high-yield bonds. Allocations are as follows:

VPLS's credit quality is quite strong, which should minimize drawdowns during downturns and recessions. It is possible for the fund to see gains during these, due to a flight-to-quality effect impacting its treasuries and MBS, and as the Federal Reserve tends to cut rates during recessions, putting upward pressure on quality bond prices.

VPLS's credit quality is somewhat weaker than that of its benchmark, which does not invest in high-yield bonds in any meaningful amount.

Due to the above, I would expect VPLS to slightly underperform its benchmark during most downturns and recessions. Active management could reduce any drawdowns here, perhaps even lead to outperformance, although that is not my base case scenario. Losses and underperformance should both be quite minor, as the differences in credit quality here are not all that significant.

With its inception in late 2023, the fund has yet to experience a meaningful downturn. Closest scenario to this is the equity bear market in early 2025, caused by bearish sentiment/the imposition of tariffs. Equity market losses barely impacted fixed-income markets, although there were small, temporary losses there, and VPLS did seem to underperform. Results are barely significant, short-lived, and could plausibly be the result of normal volatility. Results are as follows:

VPLS's rate risk is moderate, with a duration of 5.6 years, not significantly different from BND's 5.7 figure. Expect moderate gains if rates were to plummet and losses if these were to increase. Do remember that VPLS is exposed to treasury, MBS, and corporate bond rates, not Federal Reserve rates per se. Significant changes to Fed rates would almost certainly have an impact on rates for these securities; smaller changes might not. As mentioned previously, VPLS is a young fund and has yet to experience a full rate cycle, so I can't really gauge its performance during one.

VPLS's credit risk is low, and rate risk is moderate. Compared to its benchmark, it has a bit more credit risk, but the differences are not all that significant. At the same time, it is more diversified on an asset class basis, which should decrease risks somewhat. Active management might do so too, although this is far from certain.

Overall, I would expect VPLS's realized volatility and drawdowns to both be slightly or marginally higher than those of its benchmark. Realized volatility seems, if anything, below average. Results here seem slightly above expectations but don't seem too surprising; see the above, and as the differences are small.

Circling back, VPLS's overall risk and volatility seem low and about average compared to its benchmark. The former is an important benefit for shareholders, the second an important consideration for the same. Active ETFs are generally riskier than its index peers, but that does not seem to be the case for VPLS, and it does merit mention.

Dividend Yield And Performance

Active bond ETFs generally sport above-average dividend yields, at least relative to their benchmarks, as their managers focus on higher-yielding securities. VPLS's 4.5% dividend yield is higher than that of its benchmark, as expected.

VPLS's dividend yield is marginally higher than that of investment-grade corporate bond benchmark ETFs, even though credit quality is arguably higher (significantly higher treasury/MBS allocations should outweigh the smaller investments in high-yield bonds, but these comparisons are not trivial to do).

Active bond ETFs might outperform their benchmarks, contingent on their managers picking the right securities and making effective decisions. Results here are much more uncertain than for the dividends because it is trivially easy to pick higher-yielding securities much harder to pick outperforming securities. VPLS has outperformed its benchmark since inception, and reasonably consistently so. Technically it has underperformed these past thirty days, but only marginally so, and the difference seems unsustainable from volatility.

Well-Balanced Advantages And Investment Thesis

Throughout the article, I've shown that VPLS's dividend yield and past returns are higher than those of its benchmark, with realized volatility comparable. Although the fund does not excel in any of these three metrics, it does do reasonably well on each, making for an incredibly well-rounded fund and investment thesis. Lots of advantages, fewer disadvantages, none significant.

VPLS - Other Considerations

I mentioned before that VPLS is quite close to its benchmark, without any significant differences. An important implication of this is that the fund's performance should, ultimately, be quite close to that of its benchmark. Slight outperformance and underperformance are both possible, and the former has occurred since inception, but significant differences in performance seem all but impossible. This is not the case for lots of ETFs, including the Eldridge BBB-B CLO ETF (CLOZ) and the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW), two I've previously covered.

Due to the above, more aggressive investors might prefer other funds. Something like TLTW could easily significantly outperform from here on out, on account of its massive 13.5% distribution yield. It could easily underperform too, on account of its massive 15.4-year duration.

Flipside of the above is that more conservative, risk-averse investments might prefer VPLS over its peers. This is particularly the case for more conservative medium- and long-term investors; short-term investors should probably focus on cash, as well as those desiring market-like returns.

Conclusion

VPLS's well-balanced portfolio and investment thesis, above-average 4.5% dividend yield, and consistent outperformance make the fund a buy. As the fund focuses on high-quality securities and does not significantly differ from its benchmark, it might be of particular interest to more risk-averse investors.

data.title: Federal Realty Trust Q4 Earnings: Malls Are Thriving
data.description:
Consistency is rare in the real estate game. A recent theme of my coverage in Seeking Alpha has been the cyclicality of real estate. I’ve recently talked about companies like Camden Property Trust (CPT) and Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN), highlighting how each is navigating cyclicality in their own ways. While both are struggling in the face of tailwinds, both have pivoted and adapted to a changing environment with differing degrees of success. Even more recently, I discussed Modiv Industrial (MDV), focusing on their recent hinting at a take-private after failing to achieve their value in the public markets. The point being, times are tough in the world of real estate.

With an increasing number of landlords sitting in the doldrums, REITs that have been able to charge forward are few and far between. Even more rare are companies that are able to stick to their core business in the face of an ever-changing world. Perhaps the best representative example of this paradigm is Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT). FRT is one of the nation’s largest mall landlords and one of the few companies to achieve a 50-year streak of rising dividends.

FRT’s reputation as the dividend king of real estate makes the company one of the more well-known publicly traded REITs. Today, we will follow up on my prior coverage of FRT and discuss 2025 earnings.

Review of Prior Coverage

Last year, I initiated coverage on FRT. This article included an overview of the REIT’s portfolio, business model, and capitalization. Today, the company has continued much down the same path, so I will keep the review brief.

FRT is one of the largest dedicated owner operators of shopping center real estate in the United States. The company owns more than 100 properties covering around 27 million square feet of retail space and over 3,000 units of multifamily. In total, the footprint of FRT's portfolio covers more than 2,100 acres. As you would expect with any shopping center owner, tenancy is diverse with more than 3,500 commercial tenants.

My last coverage of FRT focused around the operating business model of mall REITs. More specifically, I talked about how being a mall landlord is much closer to running a traditional business than running a triple net REIT like Agree Realty (ADC). The business is more capital intensive and requires a more hands-on approach. Margins are more sensitive to changes in the cost structure, and there are more opportunities to generate revenue through ancillary sources like parking and advertising revenue. While this highlights opportunity for value add via creativity, it opens the REIT to a host of new issues. Suddenly, short-term changes in consumer preference and demand become impactful to the REIT’s top and bottom lines.

For FRT, this means a business that is inherently less predictable and more susceptible to year-over-year change. From this perspective, FRT’s track record of rising dividends is even more unusual. For instance, compare FRT’s dividend payments to competitors like Simon Property Group (SPG) or Macerich (MAC).

My prior coverage also outlined how FRT has managed to benefit from the other side of real estate’s cyclicality. Since publication, FRT has performed well on solid retail demand. FRT has outperformed real estate (VNQ) and the S&P 500.

Earnings Review

FRT recently reported full-year earnings for 2025. On the back of remarkable strength in the shopping center market, FRT delivered remarkably strong results. Top-line and per-share results grew significantly, and the company delivered some encouraging tidbits around their business. Management gave their feedback on the earnings call.

Strong quarter, strong year, strong 2026 guidance, 6.4% bottom line FFO growth in the quarter, 4.3% for the year and guidance close to 6% at the midpoint for 2026.

Along the theme of FRT being a REIT with an operational business, it should not surprise us that leasing velocity and activity account for a powerful portion of earnings. While other REITs depend heavily on acquiring or building new assets, a significant portion of FRT’s value is delivered through adding value within their portfolio. Over the year, FRT leased $11 million worth of annualized base rent.

No surprise that leasing drives these and future results. With 601,000 feet of comparable deals done in the quarter at 12% rollover and 2.3 million feet of comparable deals done for the year at 15% rollover, an incremental $11 million of new rent is under contract.

As FRT continues to add value through lease-up and redevelopment of their existing assets, the company also has a value-add component. Right now, FRT is looking to begin engaging one of their smaller, ancillary business units of developing residential at their properties. Right now, FRT is adding multifamily to several assets, including Blayr at Bala Cynwyd.

We've previously disclosed the allocation of a total of $280 million for new residential development of the Blayr at Bala Cynwyd, which is nearly complete and ready for lease-up beginning this quarter, 301 Washington Street, Hoboken and Lot 12 at Santana Row, which together will add more than 500 units to the portfolio. And just this quarter, we've added another residential project to our development schedule that you can see in the 8-K. Willow Grove Shopping Center in suburban Philadelphia will be completely redeveloped and include an additional 261 apartments to complement a modernized and remerchandised shopping center.

Our experience with residential development at our retail-centric properties is a skill set developed over 25 years and is certainly a unique differentiator of our business plan. After enjoying the 6.5% to 7% or higher income contribution from each of these residential additions for a period of time, we have the optionality to take advantage of cap rates well inside those yields and reinvest them tax efficiently, just as we've done so effectively last year and this.

Creativity is the name of the game for FRT. When the sun is shining, FRT needs to make hay and capitalize on tailwinds. If we rewind to five years ago, FRT was in survival mode. Post-pandemic and staring down Amazon (AMZN), the boogeyman of retail. Bankruptcies were booming, and nobody imagined pouring capital into building a new shopping center. Fast forward, and retail made a comeback. The lack of new supply coming online over the past five years means an undersupplied and growing market is helping FRT deliver strong results.

Right now, things look bright, and FRT is making the most of it.

Outlook and Risks

As I mentioned, FRT is a relatively sensitive business for a REIT. The company is forced to manage their portfolio and commit capital on a speculative basis to keep their assets in top shape and add value. This is often difficult for a mall operator who is forced to predict consumer trends and macroeconomic changes simultaneously.

Add in more uncertainty around economic policy like tariffs and trade, and the outlook becomes even more murky. At the end of the day, FRT is powered by people going to their malls and spending money at stores and restaurants. This means economic activity is the main success driver for FRT, like many other companies.

Based on market review from Cushman & Wakefield, the shopping center segment has performed well over the past year.

Retail market fundamentals improved over the course of 2025, driven by limited new supply, robust backfilling activity, and reduced uncertainty around tariffs and consumer spending. These dynamics translated into stronger demand in Q4, as the U.S. economy absorbed 3.4 msf of retail space—the strongest quarterly performance since Q4 2023. After three quarters of weakness, every region posted positive Q4 absorption. The Midwest led with 1.2 msf followed by the West with 1.0 msf, the Northeast with 638,000 sf, and the South with 589,000 sf. Despite this late-year rebound, all regions remained negative for 2025 overall. Shopping center vacancies remain near historic lows. For context, pre-pandemic vacancy rates typically hovered around 7%, making today’s 5.7% especially tight despite absorption volatility experienced throughout 2024 and early 2025. High quality retail space remains scarce in most markets.

Recently, I’ve been highlighting what I see as stress within private credit and private equity. Rising nonaccruals and delayed exits of some of the hotter names have led to some jitters around the near- and mid-term economic outlooks. FRT is a secondary participant in these markets, often depending on sponsored businesses in the retail industry to pay rent. If bankruptcies are to begin appearing and there is another retail-pocalypse, FRT and other mall REITs may find themselves in trouble.

Over the past few years, rising costs have increasingly pressured the consumer. This past holiday season, spending was impacted as families were forced to reconsider their budgeting. Going further, shopping online is still a growing threat as online shopping continues to grow as a portion of total retail sales. However, looking at the big picture, the boogeyman of online shopping has still taken a relatively small bite out of the enormous retail industry.

After all, the threat of AMZN was enough to begin cracking confidence in some of the largest mall landlords.

FRT and similar owners have a degree of insulation through the experiential component of their properties. Going to the mall remains a desirable experience for people across demographic groups, despite volatility in shorter-term trends.

Conclusion

From a long-term perspective, FRT is one of the most well-positioned retail REITs. While their business is inherently capital intensive, popular malls are goldmines for generating percentage rent through rising foot traffic. As FRT continues to reinvest in its assets, the company should be able to stay ahead of the next generation of retail trends. Following a run for FRT shares over several months, the company earns a Hold rating.
